content.text: (SK) is Microsoft's open-source AI SDK for Python, C#, and Java. Its primary abstraction is the

— a dependency-injection container that registers AI services and plugins, then orchestrates calls between them. SK emphasises enterprise integration: it ships adapters for Azure OpenAI, Azure AI Services, and Microsoft 365 Copilot out of the box.
on PyPI) targets Python 3.10+ and is async-first. Every AI call is a coroutine, and the Kernel's function invocation pipeline is built around

pip install semantic-kernel
# OpenAI connector included; for Azure OpenAI:
# pip install semantic-kernel[azure]
and register at least one AI service. The service you register determines which AI capabilities are available.

import asyncio
from semantic_kernel import Kernel
from semantic_kernel.connectors.ai.open_ai import OpenAIChatCompletion

kernel = Kernel()

kernel.add_service(
    OpenAIChatCompletion(
        service_id="gpt4o",          # logical name used when invoking
        ai_model_id="gpt-4o",
        api_key="sk-...",            # or set OPENAI_API_KEY env var
    )
)
and supply the endpoint and deployment name instead of the model ID.
A prompt function is an LLM call backed by a template string. Create one inline with
and invoke it via the Kernel.

import asyncio
from semantic_kernel import Kernel
from semantic_kernel.functions import KernelFunctionFromPrompt, KernelArguments
from semantic_kernel.connectors.ai.open_ai import OpenAIChatCompletion

kernel = Kernel()
kernel.add_service(OpenAIChatCompletion(service_id="gpt4o", ai_model_id="gpt-4o"))

summarise = KernelFunctionFromPrompt(
    function_name="summarise",
    plugin_name="TextUtils",
    prompt="Summarise the following text in one sentence:

{{$input}}",
    description="Summarise a piece of text.",
)

async def main():
    result = await kernel.invoke(
        summarise,
        KernelArguments(input="Semantic Kernel is a lightweight SDK that helps developers build AI applications.")
    )
    print(result)   # "Semantic Kernel is a lightweight SDK for AI app development."

asyncio.run(main())
syntax (Handlebars-style). Every variable must be passed via
to register it as a callable native function alongside prompt functions.

import asyncio
from semantic_kernel import Kernel
from semantic_kernel.functions import kernel_function, KernelArguments
from semantic_kernel.connectors.ai.open_ai import OpenAIChatCompletion

class MathPlugin:
    @kernel_function(description="Multiply two numbers.")
    def multiply(self, a: float, b: float) -> float:
        return a * b

    @kernel_function(description="Add two numbers.")
    def add(self, a: float, b: float) -> float:
        return a + b

kernel = Kernel()
kernel.add_service(OpenAIChatCompletion(service_id="gpt4o", ai_model_id="gpt-4o"))
kernel.add_plugin(MathPlugin(), plugin_name="Math")

async def main():
    result = await kernel.invoke(
        kernel.plugins["Math"]["multiply"],
        KernelArguments(a=12.0, b=7.0)
    )
    print(result)   # 84.0

asyncio.run(main())
is a typed dict that carries both template variables and model-level settings.

from semantic_kernel.functions import KernelArguments
from semantic_kernel.connectors.ai.open_ai import OpenAIChatPromptExecutionSettings

settings = OpenAIChatPromptExecutionSettings(
    service_id="gpt4o",
    temperature=0.3,
    max_tokens=512,
    top_p=0.95,
)

args = KernelArguments(
    input="Explain retrieval-augmented generation.",
    settings=settings,
)

result = await kernel.invoke(summarise, args)
object and pass it to the chat completion service directly.

import asyncio
from semantic_kernel import Kernel
from semantic_kernel.connectors.ai.open_ai import OpenAIChatCompletion
from semantic_kernel.contents import ChatHistory

kernel = Kernel()
service = OpenAIChatCompletion(service_id="gpt4o", ai_model_id="gpt-4o")
kernel.add_service(service)

async def main():
    chat: ChatHistory = ChatHistory()
    chat.add_system_message("You are a concise Python tutor.")

    chat.add_user_message("What is a decorator in Python?")
    reply = await service.get_chat_message_content(chat_history=chat, settings=None)
    chat.add_message(reply)
    print(f"Bot: {reply}")

    chat.add_user_message("Can you show a simple example?")
    reply = await service.get_chat_message_content(chat_history=chat, settings=None)
    print(f"Bot: {reply}")

asyncio.run(main())

import asyncio
from semantic_kernel import Kernel
from semantic_kernel.connectors.ai.open_ai import OpenAIChatCompletion
from semantic_kernel.contents import ChatHistory

kernel = Kernel()
service = OpenAIChatCompletion(service_id="gpt4o", ai_model_id="gpt-4o")
kernel.add_service(service)

async def main():
    chat = ChatHistory()
    chat.add_user_message("Explain async/await in Python in detail.")

    async for chunk in service.get_streaming_chat_message_content(
        chat_history=chat,
        settings=None,
    ):
        print(chunk, end="", flush=True)
    print()

asyncio.run(main())
The Kernel routes every function call through an invocation pipeline that supports pre/post hooks (
). Use filters for logging, caching, rate-limiting, and validation without modifying the function itself.

from semantic_kernel import Kernel
from semantic_kernel.filters import FunctionInvocationContext

async def logging_filter(context: FunctionInvocationContext, next):
    print(f"[INVOKE] {context.function.plugin_name}.{context.function.name}")
    await next(context)
    print(f"[RESULT] {context.result}")

kernel = Kernel()
kernel.add_function_invocation_filter(logging_filter)
When a prompt template is rendered
When the model triggers automatic function calling
Guardrails, rate limiting per tool call
The Kernel acts as a DI container. Register services with
by interface type or service ID.

from semantic_kernel.connectors.ai.open_ai import OpenAIChatCompletion
from semantic_kernel.connectors.ai.chat_completion_client_base import ChatCompletionClientBase

# Register two services
kernel.add_service(OpenAIChatCompletion(service_id="fast", ai_model_id="gpt-4o-mini"))
kernel.add_service(OpenAIChatCompletion(service_id="powerful", ai_model_id="gpt-4o"))

# Retrieve by service_id
fast_service = kernel.get_service("fast", type=ChatCompletionClientBase)
Plugins group related kernel functions into a cohesive unit. Any class whose methods are decorated with
can be registered as a plugin.
Plugin names must be unique within a kernel. Functions can be invoked programmatically via
strings — they are surfaced to LLM planners and auto-selection logic.
Semantic Kernel integrates vector memory backends so prompts and native functions can retrieve context by similarity rather than exact key lookup.

from semantic_kernel import Kernel
from semantic_kernel.connectors.memory.chroma import ChromaMemoryStore
from semantic_kernel.memory import SemanticTextMemory, VectorStoreTextSearch

kernel = Kernel()

# Configure an embedding service
from semantic_kernel.connectors.ai.open_ai import OpenAITextEmbedding
kernel.add_service(OpenAITextEmbedding(service_id="embed", ai_model_id="text-embedding-3-small"))

# Use Chroma as the backing store
store = ChromaMemoryStore(persist_directory="./chroma_db")
memory = SemanticTextMemory(storage=store, embeddings_generator=kernel.get_service("embed"))

# Save a fact
await memory.save_information(collection="facts", id="fact1",
    text="The Semantic Kernel SDK supports Python, C#, and Java.")

# Retrieve semantically similar facts
results = await memory.search("facts", "Which languages does SK support?")
for r in results:
    print(r.text, r.relevance)
abstraction wraps any store (Chroma, Azure AI Search, Pinecone, Redis) behind a common interface. Change the store without changing the rest of the code.
When a chat completion model supports tool-use, Semantic Kernel can automatically discover and invoke kernel functions to satisfy the model's requests — no manual loop required.

from semantic_kernel import Kernel
from semantic_kernel.connectors.ai.open_ai import OpenAIChatCompletion
from semantic_kernel.connectors.ai.function_choice_behavior import FunctionChoiceBehavior
from semantic_kernel.connectors.ai.open_ai import OpenAIChatPromptExecutionSettings

kernel = Kernel()
kernel.add_service(OpenAIChatCompletion(service_id="chat", ai_model_id="gpt-4o"))
kernel.add_plugin(MathPlugin(), plugin_name="math")   # from earlier example

settings = OpenAIChatPromptExecutionSettings(
    service_id="chat",
    function_choice_behavior=FunctionChoiceBehavior.Auto(),  # let the model call tools
)

from semantic_kernel.contents import ChatHistory
chat = ChatHistory()
chat.add_user_message("What is the square root of 144?")

response = await kernel.invoke_prompt(
    "{{$chat_history}}",
    chat_history=chat,
    settings=settings,
)
print(response)   # "The square root of 144 is 12."
tells the kernel to pass all registered functions to the model. The model emits a tool-call response; the kernel executes the function and injects the result before continuing — all transparently.
Planners decompose a high-level goal into an ordered sequence of function calls. Semantic Kernel ships two built-in planner types.
Generates a linear plan as XML then executes each step
Creates a Handlebars template that calls SK functions inline
Uses native LLM tool-call to dynamically select functions
Planners call the LLM multiple times per goal. Use them for complex goals; for simple single-step tasks, call
LLM API calls can fail transiently. Semantic Kernel propagates exceptions as standard Python exceptions so you can wrap calls in normal try/except blocks or use a retry library.
wraps errors from both the LLM service and from native function exceptions. Check
for the underlying cause (e.g., an HTTP 429 rate-limit).
Semantic Kernel vs Other Agent Frameworks

Choose Semantic Kernel when building on Azure or integrating into Microsoft-ecosystem products (Teams bots, Copilot extensions). Its first-class C# support and enterprise patterns (DI container, filters, planners) suit production .NET workloads.
is the central orchestrator — it holds services, plugins, and the invocation pipeline
creates an LLM call from a template;
exposes native Python methods as callable functions
carries both template variables and model settings (
manages multi-turn conversation; pass it to the chat completion service directly for stateful conversations
Function invocation filters intercept every kernel call for logging, caching, and guardrails without modifying function code
